does: This survey comprehensively reviews machine unlearning techniques specifically designed for generative AI systems including large language models and vision models. It establishes new problem formulations tailored to generative architectures, catalogs evaluation methodologies for assessing unlearning effectiveness, and systematically compares different unlearning approaches—examining their respective advantages and limitations. The work addresses a critical gap by recognizing that unlearning methods developed for traditional classification tasks fail to adequately handle the memorization and potential generation of sensitive, biased, or harmful information that emerges from web-scale training data in generative models. The survey synthesizes current research directions and identifies key technical and practical challenges in the unlearning space.
connects: This work builds on foundational machine unlearning research that emerged in response to privacy and data-removal requirements, extending those concepts to the generative AI paradigm where models exhibit qualitatively different memorization behaviors and generation capabilities. It responds to growing concerns about large language models and vision models reproducing training data verbatim or generating biased content, connecting to parallel work on model bias, hallucination, and privacy in deep learning. The survey synthesizes insights from data deletion frameworks, knowledge editing approaches, and fine-tuning-based unlearning, while acknowledging why these traditional methods prove insufficient for generative systems. It builds on empirical findings showing that large generative models memorize substantial portions of their training corpora in ways that differ from classifier architectures.
enables: This survey establishes a structured foundation for developing more effective unlearning techniques tailored to generative AI's unique architectural and behavioral properties. It enables future research by clarifying evaluation metrics and benchmarks specific to generative models, supporting development of scalable unlearning methods that can handle model scale and parameter efficiency. The work points toward practical applications in compliance with data removal regulations, mitigation of harmful content generation, and bias correction in deployed systems. By systematizing the challenge landscape and charting open problems, it facilitates interdisciplinary efforts combining privacy, interpretability, and model editing perspectives. The curated reading list and problem formulations provide a foundation for community-driven advances in making generative AI systems more controllable, safer, and legally compliant.
